ontology.foodex2_description: Papadum — lentil-based thin crisp wafer, spiced
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for chilli-topped papad; A0FKP (flatbread/cracker category) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for papadum or chilli papad as of 2026.

preparation_method: topped with fresh chilli, onion, tomato, and chaat masala

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chilli papad is one piece (approximately 20–25g), often served as a starter or accompaniment at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Papadum, plain, uncooked — Singapore HPB Foodo / ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for papadum/papad entry (cr_id G00010759). Values align with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for urad dal-based papad. Sugar reported as 0.0g/100g is consistent with lentil-based cracker formulation — confirmed zero per HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of lentil-based foods — papadum analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (lentil/urad dal cracker category)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for chilli papad in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 estimated from the closest analogous food: plain papadum / urad dal-based lentil cracker, which has a published GI of approximately 48 (low GI category) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for lentil-based flatbreads and crackers. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (48) × total carbohydrate per serving (22g × 59.9/100 = 13.18g) / 100 ≈ 6.3 GL per 22g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1740mg/100g — critically elevated; a single 22g serving delivers approximately 383mg sodium, exceeding 25% of the 1500mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid chilli papad due to its extremely high sodium content (1740mg/100g); if consumed, restrict to one small piece (≤15g) and avoid additional salt or chaat masala toppings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total carbohydrate 59.9g/100g partially offset by high dietary fibre 18.6g/100g; low GI (~48) from urad dal base reduces acute glycaemic impact, but portion control remains essential.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume chilli papad in small portions (one piece, ~20g) as the high fibre content and low GI of the lentil base moderate the glycaemic response; roasted preparation is preferred over fried to avoid fat-driven insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1740mg/100g poses significant cardiovascular risk; fat is low (3.25g/100g) and cholesterol is minimal (4mg/100g), but the sodium burden dominates the cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit chilli papad due to its critically high sodium content, which elevates blood pressure and increases cardiovascular event risk; high potassium (1000mg/100g) and fibre (18.6g/100g) are positive but do not offset the sodium hazard at typical serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1740mg/100g worsens tissue oedema and inflammation; nightshade ingredients (green chilli, tomato) may trigger solanine-related joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chilli papad due to critically high sodium and the presence of nightshade vegetables (chilli, tomato) that may aggravate joint inflammation in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Papad is a popular Indian snack and appetiser consisting of a thin, crispy lentil wafer (papad or papadum) made primarily from black gram (urad dal) flour, topped with finely chopped green chilli, tomato, onion, and chaat masala spice blend. It is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore as a starter or accompaniment to main meals. The base papad is typically roasted or fried until light and crisp, then dressed with fresh toppings that add heat, acidity, and crunch. Nutritionally, chilli papad is notable for its high protein content (25.6g/100g) and exceptional dietary fibre (18.6g/100g) derived from the urad dal base, making it relatively satiating for a snack. However, its sodium content is critically high at 1740mg/100g, driven by added salt and chaat masala, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The carbohydrate content (59.9g/100g) is partially offset by the high fibre, and the low glycaemic index (~48) means blood glucose impact is moderated when consumed in small portions. Iron (7.8mg/100g), magnesium (271mg/100g), potassium (1000mg/100g), and calcium (143mg/100g) are present in meaningful quantities. Chilli papad is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is typically consumed in small quantities (15–25g per piece) as a starter or side dish at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in papad?
Is papad good for weight loss?
What is chilli papad made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010759) with confidence 0.95 across all macros and micronutrients. Values are consistent with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for urad dal-based papad. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for lentil-based crackers, reducing GI confidence to medium; overall record confidence remains high due to strong macro and micronutrient provenance.
notes: 1. sugar_g is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; urad dal-based papad contains no added or intrinsic sugars. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific chilli papad preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables as of 2026; these sub-fractions are not available in either approved source for this food entry. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: individual vitamin data for this chilli papad preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010759) or the ICMR-NIN tables for papad as of 2026; no approved source provides these values for this specific food. 4. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry or ICMR-NIN tables for papad as of 2026; no approved source provides these values for this specific food. 5. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this chilli papad preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN tables as of 2026. 6. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.5g/100g based on ICMR-NIN typical values for urad dal papad with minimal oil; this is an approximation. 7. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for lentil/urad dal-based crackers; no direct GI entry exists for chilli papad. 8. Food group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives / Chicken' to 'Snacks / Savoury snacks and crackers' — chilli papad is a lentil-based cracker snack, not a meat or chicken product; the original classification appears to be a data entry error. 9. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible anchor for flatbread/cracker category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for papadum.
